serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Methi as a side dish in a Singaporean Indian meal is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate ladle portion served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Methi — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Methi (cr_id G00010861). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate spiced meat curry with fenugreek
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Methi. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate spiced meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Fenugreek (methi) is documented in peer-reviewed literature (Neelakantan et al., 2010, British Journal of Nutrition) to have hypoglycaemic and GI-lowering properties, supporting a low GI classification. Carbohydrate content is 7.44 g/100g; GL per 150 g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g exceeds the recommended threshold for hypertension-safe foods (≤400 mg/100g); potassium at 210 mg/100g provides only partial offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g; pairing with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach can further improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g), high protein (14.05 g/100g), and fenugreek's evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties collectively support a low glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Methi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 150 g portion; avoid pairing with large servings of white rice or naan, and monitor blood glucose if on hypoglycaemic medication as fenugreek may potentiate drug effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 85 mg/100g and total fat at 11.58 g/100g are moderate concerns; saturated fat breakdown is not reported in the HPB source, limiting precise CVD risk stratification.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Methi to a 100–120 g portion and choose lean cuts of mutton; the fenugreek component provides beneficial soluble fibre and phytochemicals that may modestly support lipid management, but the dish should not replace lower-fat protein sources in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid, a direct precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids that can exacerbate rheumatoid arthritis symptoms.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting mutton with chicken or oily fish to retain the anti-inflammatory benefit of fenugreek while eliminating the primary pro-inflammatory red meat component.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Methi is a spiced Indian curry dish made from boneless mutton (goat meat) slow-cooked with fresh or dried fenugreek leaves (methi), onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, red chilli, and a blend of aromatic spices. The dish is a staple of Indian Muslim and North Indian cuisine and is widely available in Singaporean Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The cooking process typically involves sautéing the aromatics and spices in oil, browning the mutton pieces, then braising the meat in a tomato-based gravy until tender, with fenugreek leaves added towards the end to preserve their distinctive slightly bitter, herbaceous flavour. The texture is tender and moist with a rich, deeply spiced gravy. Nutritionally, Mutton Methi provides 192 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 14.05 g, moderate fat of 11.58 g, and low carbohydrate of 7.44 g, making it a protein-dense, low-glycaemic dish. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100g, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension. Fenugreek (methi) is clinically recognised for its hypoglycaemic properties, making this dish particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice, roti prata, or naan at lunch or dinner.

The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database supported by peer-reviewed fenugreek literature, which is the maximum achievable evidence level for this dish.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Mutton Methi (G00010861) and are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for an exact Mutton Methi preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. Input description stated 'Boneless chicken cooked with fenugreek' — this is an error in the source record; the food is correctly identified as Mutton Methi (boneless mutton with fenugreek) based on the food name, food_subgroup, and HPB data. The description has been corrected in this output. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Methi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimation is based on low-carbohydrate spiced meat curry analogues and peer-reviewed fenugreek hypoglycaemic evidence (Neelakantan et al., 2010, British Journal of Nutrition). 4. Halal status is inferred from the dish being a traditional Indian Muslim preparation; kosher status is not applicable. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
